speaker: interviewer
text: Fira, we'll finish off here, I think. Can you tell me-- it's been 50 years since the end of the war and do you have any messages or feelings that you would like to pass on to your future generations?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm glad I decide to do it. I was influenced by my family. And the message, I-- by doing this visual history, I describe my feelings, and my fears, and my nightmares, and I-- my plea to the-- to the world and to all the people to remember and learn from the past so that it will never happen again.

speaker: interviewer
text: On behalf of myself, I'm glad that you did it too. Really, thank you, and on behalf of the Foundation-- [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: --thank you. Thank you for sharing your story. It's a very important story.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know if it's such important. People, maybe went through more, more, but for me, is is-- it is also a past.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's a very emotional past, and thank you for sharing it with us, really. Fira, who's in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my parents-- my mother and father.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name is Moshe. My mother's name is Hinde.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was that taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in the Chelm Park.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the outfit that your mother was wearing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was olive green, two pieces, and a black velvet hat, and my father's was a navy suit.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in a photographer's place.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how old were you there?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the dress that you were wearing?

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a color photo always?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, it was one of the first colored photos.

speaker: interviewee
text: In this picture, I am the dark dress--

speaker: interviewee
text: --Front on the right. And they're my school girlfriends, which none of them survived.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was also in the park.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was is in think 1937, I think.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, on the right, that's myself, and my sister Malka--

speaker: interviewee
text: --and my brother Daniel, and my older brother Bayer.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was on a holiday just before the war broke out on a, how you say, kiosk.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, that was myself and my sister Malka. It's also at the same time--

speaker: interviewee
text: --on holiday. The one behind is my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was in which town?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's was also on the holiday.

speaker: interviewee
text: That is my sister Malka. That was sent to us to Russia in 1941-- one of the last letters.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, that was after we got married in Russia, [INAUDIBLE] photo [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that what you wore when you got married? Is that the dress that you wore when you got married?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, that was later. I made one.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the right is my sister Sarah. Behind is her husband.

speaker: interviewee
text: Meilech, and the little-- with the wife, that's her son [? Henrich, ?] and the other one is a son David, and then is my daughter Ann, myself, and my husband.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, that's my sister came for a visit to Wetzlar.

speaker: interviewee
text: --from Berlin. That's one of the right.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's Sarah? Sarah, and then my brother and myself. And your brother's name? Bayer. And what was your sister's surname?

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was taken in Wetzlar?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, that's my brother, Bayer. He lived-- that was taken in Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my sister, Sarah. That was in 1957.

speaker: interviewee
text: She came-- yes. That most probably was made for a passport-- passport photo. That was Jeff's-- my son's-- bar mitzvah. On the right is my husband's sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: Gusta Goldberger and her husband, Henrik Goldberger, and my sister Sarah Akerstein, and Meilech Ackerstein.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you tell me what year that was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was a [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] reception after the bar mitzvah.

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister passed away two years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about this picture, Fira.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my daughter. Ann went to visit where she comes from, and she went to visit. That's where I lived before the war. That was Chelm and [INAUDIBLE] 18.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was this picture taken? In what year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a wedding photo from my granddaughter, Sharon and her husband Stephen. And what's their surname? Pelcer. Where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I don't know. She's married already. I don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: These are my two great-grandchildrens. That's Sharon's children, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Natasha and Jesse--

speaker: interviewee
text: In, I think, in [? Moruya ?] in Batemans Bay.

speaker: interviewer
text: Fira, can you introduce the gathering that's here now, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, that's my husband Moniek, with our granddaughter--

speaker: interviewee
text: --Michaela, and that's my daughter Ann, and my grandson--

speaker: interviewee
text: --George, and that's my other grandson Ian, and my daughter-in-law--

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, the two children belong to?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my son' wife. That's their two children. And that's Ann's husband, and son Ian, and a daughter Sharon, and--

speaker: interviewee
text: She's in the country with two children, Natasha and Jesse, and husband Steven.

speaker: interviewer
text: We've heard quite a moving story in the last couple of hours. Anybody here want to say something about this project or about Fira and her willingness to partake in this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, well,  I-- what I was going to say was how happy I am that they've participated in this program, because I've been at them for ages to do it, and it's a wonderful way of leaving this legacy for us of your journey here, and look at all of us here today after that-- what you went through.

speaker: interviewee
text: I agree. I think it's wonderful that they been able to do this so that my children will be able to reflect on the experience and not forget what's happened. And just happy to say that even though it was a terrible experience for my mother and for my father as well, but they've come through it reasonably well-adjusted. And hopefully the kids and the family and the rest of them benefit from the tragedy that  they went through, as well. I'm happy to have them as parents--

speaker: interviewee
text: And great-grandparents. And I think it's wonderful that after, you know, the history of what you've just given--

speaker: interviewee
text: --here, that-- that, you know, you've left this for us and for the children to have. And it's fantastic that you've done it, and I hope Dad does it soon too, because you've got--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. We'll talk to him about that. Ian, as a grandson, who can represent the grandchildren [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: You'll be the spokesman for the grandchildren. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it's great that they've done it too. I mean, I had to do a project at school, and I interviewed Nana about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And that was in year 10. I had to do a project, and it was on the Holocaust, and I had to do-- interview someone. And I thought, well, grandmother's the one, so I had a good-- it's good she's doing it. It's in the archives, so I'm happy.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I'm very happy that Fira found the initiative to do this. I-- my side of my family was totally different. My parents came from Austria, from Vienna, and got out in 1938, and came to Australia, and I was born here. And there was a lot that I didn't know. My parents didn't discuss much. They-- being here during the whole Holocaust situation protected them, I guess, to a certain degree, although I know my grandparents passed away in the war. But even my parents are not 100% sure what happened to them at all.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we-- I led a fairly sheltered existence to the Holocaust and the situations. And being brought up here in Australia, which in that particular time when I was able to understand what was going on, there was no problems here. There was no anti-Semitism. There was no problems in Australia. And those people that did suffer didn't speak much about it, and I know that all my parents' friends were able to come out here.

speaker: interviewee
text: And last year, Ann and I-- or two years ago, Ann and I decided go back to Poland, or go to Poland at least, and go to where Fira was born and lived, and where Moniek lived, and it was a total eye-opening experience for us. I mean, we went, obviously, and had a look at the camps and everything, which they in themselves was horrific. But to go back and actually be in the same house or on the same street as these two and trace their journey back to Australia, it was really, I think, for Ann and I, a turning point in our lives towards the attitude that we had. We led, and we have been fortunate to lead, a very sheltered life in this country, and if this hadn't taken place, there's a lot that would not have been spoken at all. So I'm very pleased that I've been able to, as part of the family, be able to understand and feel the whole situation.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it's very hard for what Fira's doing, because it takes a lot out of her and a lot out of Moniek. I think, as children-- and obviously, I'm a child of a survivor as well-- I think it's important that we should be very proud of our heritage, not deny it, and not let it perish with those of us who are still around.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in terms of the  emotion, I think it's hard, but I think it's critical, because if we're going to be Jewish, we should stand up and say that we're proud to be Jewish, and that what happened to us is not something that should be forgotten, and it shouldn't happen again. And I think this is the only way that we can ensure that is by stating, from the people who it happened to, what happened, how it happened, and then it  becomes a living memory and not something that we forget about. So my mom's history was different, and obviously my father's history is different, but I think that everybody should be proud that they're here today. And that as children, and having children, I don't think that we should ever let it go. Moniek?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Anyway, we went a lot through during the war, me and my wife, and [INAUDIBLE] probably you know all the story, but I am very proud of my family. They're all here and we are very happy together. And my dear wife done this job, which I'm very proud of it, and maybe next time I will do this similar things. And well, I hope it won't happen again, the Holocaust won't come again.

speaker: interviewer
text: And let me finish it off, because I'm in the privileged position of saying that I've known the family most of my life in Australia anyway, and it's been a special privilege for me to be part of this, and thank you from myself and thank you on behalf of the Foundation.

speaker: interviewee
text: And thank you, Mary, for doing this job. I know it's not job, but we are very grateful to you that you work out with us and--

speaker: interviewee
text: And also, I guess, thank you to Steven Spielberg for his--

speaker: interviewee
text: --set this up so that we are able to do this and you were able to do this for us.
